author: @Johny40Se7en
text:
Wonderful tech that Archimedes screw. It was first used as a simple water pump to move water in the opposite direction of what was shown here.
7:07 in England you've had a lot of rain? LOL! Come over the bridge to Wales and see the amount of shitty rain we have, especially during Winter. In the last month, we've had about one clear day when we saw the sun, the rest have been some overcast and mostly heavy rain. 
Just imagine if you had one or two of those in the rainwater pipes coming from the roof to the storm drains and instead of powering anything directly, have the generator transfer the energy into a battery system like one of those Tesla power walls. People in rainy places would be set during wet seasons 🙃😜

replies.author: @NM-wd7kx
replies.text:
@bikerfirefarter7280 so, not quite what OP suggested, but if you set up a tiny, wind driven pump to lift the water to create a header tank, with a siphon system.
It might take a day to fill & dump, but the power output should be significantly better coming in a surge rather than a weak trickle

replies.author: @bikerfirefarter7280
replies.text:
@NM-wd7kx Nope. Power is power. Stored/discharged or used continuously, its still the same amount. Plus you'll always lose some converting it. The only thing that helps is finding the most efficient converter. Of course sometimes end use is more desirable than efficiency.
You could use a wind driven device, geared appropriately,  to lift the whole header tank while it's full of water, and then use it as a gravity battery; depending on overall efficiency/friction losses.

replies.author: @NM-wd7kx
replies.text:
@bikerfirefarter7280 there's always some question of efficiency of conversion, if using a dump tank then you could be using a heavier/larger screw, which can drive a higher torque generator, this can also be used whether or not there is heavy rain.
Whereas a light turbine might choke on a downpour & a heavy one likely won't be turned by a bit of drizzle

Would this be a viable way to produce a turbine generator that could compete production cost and operating cost with an internal combustion generator?

author: @petehiggins33
text:
Let’s do a little thought experiment shall we?

Let’s assume we collect all the rain from the roof of our two storey detached house which has a floor area of 60 m² and drop it down a drainpipe to the ground for a whole year.  How much energy would it deliver if we could recover it with 100% efficiency?

Typical annual rainfall in the UK is about one metre.  If you took the roof off your house it would fill up with rain to about waist high.  That sounds like a lot to me but that’s what the Met Office says.

The total amount of water harvested is therefore 60m² x 1m = 60 cubic metres which has a mass of 60 tons or 60,000 kg.  Good job we aren’t storing it on the roof.

If we drop this say 6 metres to the ground the resulting potential energy (E) released is the mass times the height times the acceleration due to gravity (g).  The value of g is  9.81m/s² lets say 10.  

So, E = 6m x 60,000kg x 10, which is 3.6 Megajoules.
3.6Mj is exactly one kilowatt hour
In one year.

Several other reasons; fish, boating, aquatic birds, etc.

author: @KeithOlson
text:
Very interesting!  Some thoughts:

1. As turning the screw depends on the *weight* of the water, a certain flow rate would be required to overcome inertia and the various frictions affecting the whole system.
2. Given those issues, the system's scale would have to be carefully calculated for optimal efficiency.
3. I forget what the mechanism is formally called, but it involves a container with a slanted side that can pivot.  The pivot point is set part-way up the slope, so that, as water fills the container, the center of gravity slowly shifts until it is past the pivot point, whereupon the entire container tips over and empties itself, then, once it is completely empty, the center of gravity naturally shifts to the other side of the pivot, so it tips back and resets itself.   A setup like that, with a large container, would allow for even small flows to produce significant--though periodic--generation.

Cheers!

replies.author: @vidard9863
replies.text:
not entirely accurate, Archimedes screws where designed to pump water at low revs, because each screw twist held a given amount of water. similarly at low flow each twist holds the same amount of water/energy, the rotation is just slowed. 

a tank with an auto siphon to feed it would probably be simpler to implement for your goal.

author: @simontemplarGB
text:
We would only be considering these measures because government is failing to keep the social contract of maintaining the infrastructure that secures our basic needs at reasonable cost. If we all have to secure our power needs by alternative means we are going to need vastly more materials than those required for centralised power production. We obviously won't all be doing it unless driven by desperation. For those that think that green power or renewables are the way forward they should consider the minerals and metals required to replace fossil fuels globally with current green alternatives. Simon Michaux, an Associate Professor of Geometallurgy at the Geological Survey of Finland has performed a study which indicates that at the present rate of production the copper required would take 189 years to produce, Nickel 400, Lithium 9920 and Germanium 229113 to select a few in the range. This will all require the use of fossil fuel in astronomically larger quantities and since oil may be expected to peak some time after Human stupidity the problem is clear.

I think the ideas on this channel are great and may well implement some of them and have great fun doing so, but I should not have to.

author: @Spedley_2142
text:
Does making it longer make much difference?  I get the impression you'd get roughly the same power from 1 section of screw as you do from 4.
If it uses gravity or under pressure then yes, I could see a longer screw being better but laid flat in a stream makes me think that other 3 sections would be counter productive.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: you get more torque from longer

replies.author: @petehiggins33
replies.text: You are right, in fact an Archimedes screw doesn't generate any more power than a simple propeller of  the same diameter.  When the first screw driven boats were made to replace paddles they used a long helical screw but soon discovered that they could shorten the screw without losing power and eventually ended up with the propeller (or screw) as we know it today.  What you really need to harvest energy from rainwater is a Pelton wheel.

replies.author: @bikerfirefarter7280
replies.text: @petehiggins33 Pelton is good if you have enough height/pressure. But for low height with good flow torque converted to high rpm is better.

author: @bikerfirefarter7280
text:
Hi, 
You don't need big bearings on a big pipe (oo-er, missus) it'll rotate quite nicely on the small shaft bearings. :-) Especially if you seal the screw/pipe together you don't waste on leakage AND then with low flow you can gear down to store torque in a mass or 'spring' to intermittently harvest energy. Or with gearing you can use the torque to haul really heavy weighs uphill, as long as you are not in a hurry. ;-)
You can do what I did and use a 6' dia coils of 3" pipe to produce tremendous low rpm torque with low flow and little or no leakage.
I used it to drag tons of soil up my steep allotment (it took a few rainy days to move a one ton box 30m (up 6m) but at least I wasn't shifting it)  ;-).  And the spent water went into the butts.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: oh I have seen large instals of this mate - to my mind it remains six or two threes which way you go - sorry about that - but I don't mind in the least you thinking different to me if that helps - cheers

replies.author: @bikerfirefarter7280
replies.text:
@ThinkingandTinkering I’ve seen some alarmingly large bearings in my time too. And it's got nothing to do with which side I bat for.
I wasn't nit picking then either, Your video just reminded me of the problem of low/intermitent rain flow and the bypass leakage.
For the coil I used, it was the force multiplier that I needed, as I was fed up shoving a barrow up a steep/slippy hill, and I'm not Sisyphus; and I had the materials laying about (bit of a collecter-maniac, me)...change/rest etc.
Like you say, 'six of one...'  
- No need for 'sorry' -.
Skin like a duck, water off a rhino's back.
It's all part of tripe's rife laughfetry. (anag,, almost, missing a 'c')  ahem.
ATB.

I wish you better luck mate.

author: @hunnybunnysheavymetalmusic6542
text:
I really appreciate you fluidly using the term "Energy Scavenger" because it is one of the much more accurate terms for what people have been raised to wrongly mislabel a 'generator'

Either 'energy scavenger' or 'energy converter' or even 'force converter' or 'power converter [could even say 'adapter'], but in general, I like to think that if a person views this as a way of scavenging energy that would otherwise be 'lost', 'wasted', missed', 'passed by'.

Where some people feel like they are going to leap up and scream at the wind, they are much more likely to be disappointed with their results than a person who comes in a bit more humbly and being thankful for what energy they can scrounge up.

As you yourself have pointed out easily as many times as how much you enjoy builder's board, its a matter of scale.

While this Archimedes's screw will work at any scale, not all of us can get a river and stick it in our back yard, but we do tend to often have rain guttering [speaking of that, some day I fully intend to install some on my own home] and, as you pointed out, potentially a whole lot of rain.

But also, if one has the space for a large barrel, or even a back yard swimming pool, which can fill up with intermittent or weak levels of rain, but have this device properly mounted and gated to its base, I have drained swimming pools before and they definitely put out a powerful surge of water!

Admittedly, not all of us can afford to build such a large reservoir of water, or even a plastic lined fish pond, or they may not have the space in their back yard, you can get used tank containers for often as little as 50 quid or so, and with a little washing up [because they had something in them before they were sold] they have an inlet at the top big enough for about any sized drain, you can put them up on some kind of posts at the 4 corners, and they tend to have a 50mm ball valve at the bottom, some even have larger valves, but they will often hold somewhere between 1000 and 1275 liters.

I have 3 of them myself which are the 1275 liter variety and can get more all day long if I wish, which I do eventually intend to capture the rain water with, once I install the guttering.

Also, with this kind of tank, you can easily put in a drop-down basket made out of that fine stainless steel mesh to make a quick easy way to strain out leaves and windblown junk that just pops into the top of the tanks and you can climb up a ladder pull the basket(s) out, shake them into a trash container or into your home organic compost pile and put them right back in! (~_^)-b

If you know any place disposing of old wine casks, those would look amazing, although depending on how they come to you, you might need a big screen on the top to keep the leaves out...

I really do not know where you would get such a barrel to be honest, even though I have seen them used in the past.

You could fashion your own barrel, but you would certainly have to engineer with for some strength, because if you don't know what you are doing, it might collapse under its own weight, and that could be pretty dangerous.

There are probably a thousand designs and hundreds of how-to' on building one's own rainwater tank systems, I've seen a few of them from out in the desert southwest where water is rare and precious, but the area they come from is fine, so long as you know such other issues as your local soil conditions.

I mention soil conditions because a lot of people fail to realize the nature of hydrodynamics and soil hydrology, put up some kind of structure, a lot of rain comes along, the soil gets soggy, and down their structure comes. And this is not just something limited to rain barrels, it has even happened to big skyscrapers in China.

If you are not sure, get some information from your local city building codes, and set your posts in plenty of concrete, and at least a full meter deep in the ground.

Even though I have [relatively] hard, tough, compact, stony, root filled, well drained soil where I live, and some times even solid bedrock, I still tend, as a general rule, for packed/tamped posts, no less than 1 meter in depth, for concrete, no less than 2/3 meter, for a 5 foot post [ 166 cm above ground ] and for 2 meter up to 255 cm, I go 133 cm tamped or 1m concreted, and for over 255 cm above ground, I I go 2m tamped or 133 cm concreted.

This is not a 'hard-and-fast' rule, just the rule I have followed under 'best practices' for fence, deck, and small structure stilt installation, and may not be sufficient where you live.

Please see your local building codes.

Also, never leave out the ever important gusseting work!

If you own a lot of land, especially on a steep slope, however, you can put in a body of water at the top of the land, with a buried drain outlet [a 'pond' or 'pool'] and a stopper-gate [see the inside  of a toilet tank for an example of those] with a simple lever to control it, and the hill height will add more potential power to your final product.

And, also, if you find you can mount a T on the upstream side, but do not want to bury your generator underground, you can always have the power take off sticking out the exit end.

Probably in such a case, you could put a simple disc with magnets and coils, like Robert showed with some of the wind barrel energy scavengers, and if one is not enough, just add more to the end of the shaft and either wire them in parallel for more amperage, or in series for more voltage.

Since they seem to require very little torque, relatively speaking, it would seem an ideal way to make use of the less robust power of a smaller system, if you lack a really large volume of water to work from.

replies.author: @hunnybunnysheavymetalmusic6542

@ThinkingandTinkering I'm used to doing a lot of technical writing.

I've done it for more than 30 years at 5 different jobs from the USAF to a geoengineering research thinktank.

I can write faster than I can speak.

And be more clearly understood too.

author: @pjclarke8298
text: Why not install it inline on your incoming water main... Be running almost constantly then with guaranteed pressure?

replies.author: @pjclarke8298
replies.text:
@ThinkingandTinkering well the pipe from the water meter into the property is owned by you the householder and not the water board...they own everything from the meter back into the road...so it would be totaly fine because it is your pipe and not their pipe...sorry if you thought I meant attach to the water main..that would be illegal....your mains water either 20mm or 25mm should come from the pavement/road at a constant guaranteed pressure (water authorities have different criteria) but I think it would be good...
If you ever have a leak in your incoming pipe you will find that it is your responsibility if it is between the meter and your house ...ie it is yours

author: @bartronicsecurity
text: Another case where instead of waiting for many hours of 3D printing you can print a rubber mold and use resin to make the parts. In fact you don't even need to glue the parts you make. All you need to do is put a small part of a finished section back in the mold and pour the resin in. It will stick to the finished part and end up as a longer piece.  Then simply repeat and grow it to a longer screw with every resin pour. Much quicker and cheaper this way.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: did you see the link at the end of the video? I made one of these with a bunch of old CDs - no need to go to the trouble of molds or muck about with resin either if you don't want and it is even cheaper!!!

replies.author: @bikerfirefarter7280
replies.text: Just use a coil of tubing, skip dive from buildings/plumbing sites  . No molds, no resin, minimal tools etc. The mech also operates from low-flow rates. With many coils you can get high torque.

author: @LoyalUK
text: just love these videos, cant stop watching, i really want to get into 3d printing but i dont really know where to start

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: that's a really nice thing to say mate - to be honest the hardest thing is making a start once you get going it is pretty easy and getting a start is easy too - the printers aren't that expensive any more and although there is certainly a lot to know to be very good at it - I made this screw as my third ever print - so doing good stuff right away just doesn't need all that much

replies.author: @LoyalUK
replies.text: @ThinkingandTinkering thank you, that's a relief i thought they were really expensive.

replies.author: @gnomish844
replies.text:
The Ender 3 is probably the most common entry point in to 3D printing. Fairly cheap, tons of folks using it so lots of information and videos out there, parts are easy to source, etc...

For your own designs, you'll need to learn some CAD software. Fusion 360s pretty popular in the community, so is TinkerCAD and Sketchup.

Start with the basics, learn as you go, and tinker on things as they need tinkered with. You got this. :)

author: @WarGrade
text: You have a dirty great river down the road! and you have some rain water gutters outside on the corner of your building, why not do a long term test on the drain if your landlord dont mind (It will go walkies if you leave it in the stour!)

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: I am not sure the rivers authorities would approve either lol

replies.author: @WarGrade
replies.text: What they don't know, don't hurt em! besides worth thinking about if you decide to do a floating power generator, lower it over the bridge and Bob's your uncle, just don't let it float off, else it will be a manic dash down stream lol

author: @TrueSighted
text: What about vertically? Would it still work if you put it directly to a downspout vertically? You'd have to waterproof the generator, but I dont think there is any reason why it wouldn't work.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: for sure - but there is no reason to and there are reasons not to

replies.author: @TrueSighted
@mrab4222
replies.text: @ThinkingandTinkering If it's vertical, surely the rainwater would have a clear path through it. I think it works better when it's at an angle and can trap water between the blades.

replies.author: @bikerfirefarter7280
replies.text:
Vertical is a no-no. However, if you put a slopped pipe on to the vertical you can run the screw in that. zig-zag the pipe back to the drain of course. 
You'd probably do ok by porting water from the vertical pipe into a hopper, and then into a dedicated larger dia screw that was sealed inside a slopping pipe, the pipe/screw arrangement being made so it all rotates. It's more a matter of extracting the potential energy from the mass/height of water falling rather than kinetic effect of flow.
Revisit the difference between p.e. and k.e.

replies.author: @mrab4222
replies.text: @bikerfirefarter7280 Well, I'm now thinking that it would work if there was large enough flow of water, so if you had a tall building and torrential rain and the downpipes were properly sealed, then maybe.

replies.author: @Michiel-S
replies.text: I was wondering if there was a way to put them into the vertical rain pipes. Allowing houses to utilise rainpower, without it being into view. Since you get 2m to 8m drops in elevation, but with a 80mm diameter

replies.author: @vidard9863
replies.text: depends on application the "less vertical"  the more water you can have acting on it for more torque with less volume, similar to a water wheel.

replies.author: @vidard9863
replies.text: @Michiel-S yes, if your generator can fit in the pipe, picture a rain pipe going to the gutter, but not quite, with a small right angle section of pipe feeding into it, from the gutter. so the generator is dry, the water comes in at a right angle beneath it, but it is not visible
